CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The question of curtailing speeches in the British
_ifouse of Commons is attracting a lot of attention just
now. But why confine the matter to the British. House
There is a disease of public speaking
rampant; thank heaven we never contracted it.  The
disease is prevalent in Irclund as well as in other places
I'he worst of public speaking is that, in most ca
length is of its essence. Who ever heard of a five

inutes’ lecture, or of an important speech delivered

minutes ? If a man is asked to lecture the re-

quest really means that lie is asked to talk for at least
an hour and a half.  But.after all, as lectures are
usually written, their effect on those addicted to them
not so harmful though it certainly does tend towards
windedness. Tt is the practice of ** standing here

ay ’ speaking, and above all the proposing, second-

ing and supporting of votes of thanks that drive people
into the habit of saying nothing at unconscionable
length. A public evening meeting in a hall must, by
convention, occupy at least two hours. A meeting
commencing at eight o’clock cannot decently be over
until ten, else, so strong is the convention, even people
who may have been bored all the time would probably:
feel that they had not got enough talk for their trouble
in attending the meeti If a man reads a good pithy
paper in half an hour—and a person with anything to
say can say a lot in that space of time—how is the
balance of time to be filled ir It is here that the silly
habit of longwinded orations cireling round votes of
thanks come in handy. It is only because people ave:
used to these votes of thanks orations from their very
childhood that they cannot see how ludicrous and silly
they are. We practically never go to feasts of public:
oratory, so we are able to take a rational and
detached view of the matter; any time we do
go to public speechifyings we either keep on
the outskirts where we not hear, or else,
except on very rare oe , feel that we
have wasted a couple of heurs listening to idle words.
And in Ireland we have got into the habit of having @
public meeting almost on as slight a provocation as
non-teetotallers ask friends to have a drink. Probably
it is as bad in England and in other countries, but that
does not detract from the ridiculousness of the habit irr
Ireland. One consequence of the custom is that it
breeds a tribe of vapid speakers who can fill any gap
and can be guaranteed on a vote of thanks, say at such
an institution as the National Literary Society, to com-
mence, if necessary, at the Flood and carry on the dis-
course to the present time. Speakers in Ireland, un-
like coals, are very cheap to-day. Even B.A.’s are
scarce in comparison with them.

We wonder will even Home Rule cure Ireland of the:
Peeler testimonial disease. ~ Why policemen, of all
men in the country, should be treated to festimonials
is one of the many inscrutable aspects of Irish char-
acter. Last week we commented on Ballinasloe, and
here now is another peeler testimonial being inflicted
on Ballyhaise in the County of Cavan. One Sergeant
McHugh has resigned ‘the Force,”” and the almost
inevitable printed circular touting for subscriptions has
been issued, and subscriptions will be received up to
June Ist. Why confine it to Ballyhaise? ~Why not
malke it a national, if not an Imperial, question?
Policernan McHugh, like all testimonial peelers, is a
most remarkable man. “He has been for years
stationed amongst us,’” says the cireular, ** and whilst
discharging his duties with efficiency, ability, excep-
tional intelligence and integrity, he did so conscien-
tiously and without in any way making himself over-
officious or offensive to any person.”’ We wonder was
he a good Bung catcher? And was he a terror or a
white-haired boy to the Sunday drinkers? How much
has Ballyhaise given to the Home Rule T'und?

A LETTER TO THE ‘‘ INDEPENDENT.

Dear *‘ Dreadful,” well knowing your pages of light

Are open to all who seek justice and right; g

I wish to bring under your notice so keer, ;

Some things that Home Rule for poor shoneens may
mean.

Oh, we, like poor downtrodden Protestant mobs,

Who never will swallow 0’Growney for jobs,

In this dawn of freedom now hailed by the erowds

See nothing but ominous shadows and clouds.

18

Oh, ““ Dreadful,” when Ireland’s no longer in thrall,
The Castle back door will be closed to us all,
And those who to royal receptions will go,
Must all be quite Irish, not English, you know.
Oh, won’t it be awful when this comes to pass

~ That toadies must look to old Ireland for class,
Must give over hanging to Sourfaces’ tails,
And copy the manners of Papists and Gaels.

And also consider, dear ‘‘ Dreadful,” what woes
On peelers and bank clerks Home Rule will impose,
Those props of provincial society green,

So loved and adored by the village shoneen.

In Home Ruling Ireland, dear ‘‘ Dreadful,”’ depend,
The green testimonial will come to an end;

And thus will a grand social structure decay

In which the great Bungs and the peelers held sway.

Then too nothing else will be seen in our halls,
- But Irish amusements and Gaclic League balls

Oh, “ Dreadful,” I tell thee, with grief I could ery,
To think that our Cockney productions will die.
Full many more prospects most gloomy indeed,

I see for poor flunkeys, but cannot proceed,

My heart with deep sorrow gets laden, and so

I'm yours very faithfully, Ballinasloe.

A. M. W.

What a look out the readers and contributors of the
LEADER keep to be sure. Last week one of our occa-
sional contributors, “‘ Setanta,’”’ discussed in a very
interesting way ‘‘ Irish Schools and School Books.”
He came down very severely from the Irish point of
view on a school book entitled ‘“ Sterling Prose and
Poetry,”” and we don’t know if the publishers have any-
thing by way of reply. But in the case of the other
book he mentioned ‘‘The School Treasury of Irish
Literature,”’ published by the Educational Company
of Ireland, Ltd., Dublin and Belfast, there is a furthér
tale to be told. We were, indeed, surprised that a
book emanating from the Educational Company of Ire-
land, entitled *“ The School Treasury of Irish Litera-
ture,” should deserve the castigation that it received
from ‘‘ Setanta,”’ viewing the matter from the Irish
point of view. ' And if we do not mistake,” wrote
vur contributor, ‘* this fantastically labelled book is one
of the first literary products of the National Univer-
sity.”’  Our contributor was quite right in his castiga-
tion of the volume before him, and yet the Educational
Company of Ireland can plead not guilty of the charges
made against their volume, *“ The School Treasury of
Irish Literature.” At once the reader will see that
there is a mystery to be solved. The solution is very
simple when you know it. We have two volumes
before us; the covers are similar, but the contents
totally different. The Educational Company of Ireland
have a very big and complex publishing business, and
X _mistakes will occur even in the best regulated
publishing businesses. A .few copies of a book
called ‘‘ Senior Reader "’ were bound up in the covers
and title pages of ‘“ The School Treasury of Irish Litera-
ture,”” and they were distributed before the error
was ‘discovered: It was a copy of the ‘‘Senior
Reader *’ in its wrong binding that came into the hands
of our contributor, and that received, from the Irish
point of view, such condemnation from him.  The
genuine ‘‘ School Treasury of Irish Literature’ is
before us now. It is a very cheap book at 9d., and the
preface tells us that * This Reader is exclusively Trish

are no fewer than twenty-four illustrations in the book,

The poor *‘ Irish Times " was reduced to incoherent
rumbling by the passing of the second reading of the
Home Rule Bill. = The chief use® which the unfortunate
man, Sir Horace Plunkett, appears to be reduced to
is to serve the purpose of scare-crow-in the fields of
the anti-Trish crowd. Sir Horace deserves a better
fate, but he allows himself to be used that way. fI‘he
“Trish Times '’ drags him into its editorial mumblings
and rumblings on the passing of the second reading.

A renegade Trishman, or man born in England of Trish
parents, one Garvin, editor of the London"' Pall Mall
(tazette ”’ and of the London ‘“ Observer,”” appears o
be the brains carrier of the Unionist Party in England.
As this man Garvin is thought so much of in Unionist
Fngland, it is interesting to read Mr. Jerry MacVeagh,
M.P., on him. Mr. MacVeagh, in the course of his
speech on the second reading of the Home Rule Bill,
said, according to the report in the “ Freeman ' :—

Mr. Garvin deserved some attention, as he was
the conscience-keeper of the Toty Party and the
policy-provider of the two Houses of Parliament.
He was now raising the voice of passion against
Ireland, but it was not ever thus. He was for a
long time the British correspondent of *‘ United
Ireland,’”’ an organ which was founded by M.
William O’Brien.  During all the time he acted
as correspondent for that paper week after week
he supported the most advanced views of Irish
Nationalism.  All that time, in the opinion of Mr.
Garvin, the Trish Party were all Whigs, soulless
creatures who could not be trusted to work the
movement to a successful issue.  In the course of
his performances Mr. Garvin had something to say
about the use of dynamite—a demand had been
made for the release of dynamite prisoners as being
political prisoners, and Mr. Garvin wrote,
*“ Whether the prisoners be guilty or not, the paci-
fication of Ireland demands their release,’’ and that
‘“ dynamite is not one whit more deadly to society
than jury-packing.”’ He attended a banquet to
celebrate the Irish Rebellion of 1798, and declared
that the system of government in Ireland was such
that, compared with it, the horrors of Armenia
““paled their ineffectual fires.”” He (Mr.
MacVeagh) had with him his Garvin dossier, the
minute book of the organization with which M.
Garvin was connected in Newcastle-on-Tyne. From
this it could be seen that Mr. Garvin took a pro-
minent part in movements to give receptions to
John Daly, who was accused of being a dynamitard,
and to O’Donovan Rossa.  On another occasion he
declared that, ‘“The business of Irishmen was
simply to watch for the whites of their enemies’
eyes and blaze away.” This was the gentleman
who, every Sunday in the ‘‘ Observer’’ and every
week-day in the ““ Pall Mall Gazette,’” assailed the
Ivish Party with all the choicest epithets in-his
vituperative vocabulary for no other crime than
that they had been true to the cause which he
himself had abandoned.

Who are the Schoolmasters’ Association ?  Whoever
they are they sent on a resolution to the Meath County
Council pointing out that of the seventeen County

error in the local Press report. What do the ** School-
masters’ Association,’’ whoever they are, want? Do
they want the County Councils to help Trinity College
out of the rates ? Surely Dublin University has already
had, and still has, sufiicient pull out of Irsh financial
resources without the County Councils adding possible
““tilleys " "cut of the rates.

What is wrong with Coachford ? Coachford, in the
County of Cork, has a Village Hall, an institution thab
one would like to see associated with every village.
But that is no reason that it should have unsatisfactory
concerts. In aid of the funds of the Coachford Village
Hall a concert was conducted by a Mr. Wilberforce
Franklin—nothing either rotten or rebel Cork about
that name. A Press report, with delightful unconscious
lumour, calls the entertainment ** a high-toned treat.””
Certainly it was as * tony * as one of the loud spoken
ladies in the Dublin tram car whose ‘“ Bertie has just
retu-ned from Switze'land.”” There is no mention of
as much as one song in Irish at this * high-toned treat,”
though Coachford is not very far from Iris| h-speaking
districts; and we believe even such a high-toned "
official as its dispensary doctor is not only an Irish
speaker but something of an Irish scholar.” What is
wrong with Coachford ?

After the storm there comes the calm. The Suffers
are very quiet now. No suffering sandwichwomen add
to the melancholy of Dublin. A few weeks ago the
Trish Women'’s Franchise League were ta king defiantly.
“‘As far as the Trish Women’s Franchise League were
concerned,”” declared one of their members, ‘‘ they
were done with the Irish Party.”” At which there were
prolonged cheers. Irish women were advised to leave
the Irish
Party. A “new phase of militancy”’ was threatened
if the Convention denied them their right to represen-
tation under the Home Rule Bill. That was all brave
talk: but now this Irish Women’s Franchise League is
cooing like a dove and has thrown aside its sandwich
boards. This wobbling and unstable league has sent a
letter through its hon. sec. to the Press. It has been
negotiating with other leagued Suffers in Ireland and a
mass meefing is to be held to demand an amendment
to the Home Rule Bill in the Suffer interest. ** Such
a demand,’’ says the letter, ‘¢ coming from combined
workers for the suffrage all over Treland, cannot, we
believe, be ignored by our members of Parliament,
especially when they have made known that they are
willing to consider amendment suggestions.””  Well,
the Suffers are not wanting in coolness, certainly; *“ our
members of Parlinment’” indeed. What about'the pro-
nouncement that ‘‘ they were done with the Irish
Party ”?  What about Mrs. Charles Hubert Oldham’s
remark, ““she told them frankly that she felt grateful

have made a mess of their case: they did their little

THE ROCHE COLLECTION OF IRISH MUSIC.

HE above ‘‘ Collection of Irish Airs, Marches
-and Dance Tunes *’ (compiled and arranged for
violin, mandoline, pipes or flute, by F. Roche, with
an Introduction by the Rev. Charles J. Brennan, pub-
lished by Messrs. Pigott and Co., Dublin, Cork and
Limerick), ‘calls for more than a passing notice. It
breaks new ground in more ways than one, and these
ways may best be indicated by what is said in the Pre-
face attached to his work by Mr. Roche himself: The
issue is *“ a handy, and at the same time comprehensive
volume of: reliable Irish-music at a moderate price.”
Few are fortunate enough to have the now rare volumes
of the Bunting and Pefrie Collections. Also the cost
of the O’Neill Collection, of Dr. Joyce’s recent monu-
mental “* Old Irvish Folk-Music and Songs,”” to say
nothing of Sir Villiers Stanford’s scholarly ‘‘ Arrange-
ments *’ (*‘ Songs of Old ‘Treland,”” ‘‘ Irish Songs and
Ballads,”” etec.), is prohibitive to the pockets of the
majority of Irish music lovers in this country. More-
over, most of these issues are bulky in size, and not
easily available for the ordinary music-stand; a point
cssential for violinists in particular. Again, regarding
the ““ reliability *’ of the tunes in the Roche Collection,
as claimed by their collector, when it is noted that Mr.
Francis Roche, who comes of a well-known musical
Limerick family, has spent some twenty years in
accumulating the national treasures which he now
offers to the public, his right to be heard as an authority
on the subject needs no question. So it is hoped that
readers will not grudge the modest 1s. 6d. a volume
which is asked for the two clearly printed and emin-
ently ““ handy " and commodious volumes containing,
in all, 854 selections, which have been issued in the
very best style by the well-known Irish firm of music
sellers and publishe Messrs. Pigott and Co., of
Dublin, Cork and Limerick.

Truly the number and versatility of our Irish folk-
songs is amazing! Dr. Grattan Flood, in his compre-
hensive ‘“ History of Irish Music,” gives an interesting
list of no fewer than 90 published collections of our
native music, Dr. P. W. Joyce estimating that, since

" this list appeared, several other large collections have

seen the light. Dr. Joyce (in his fine issue already
referred to, ““ Old Irish I'olk-Music and Songs’’), fur-
ther calculates that, even allowing for the repetitions
of airs and versions of airs in these numerous editions,
we can boast a ‘‘ safe sum-total of about 3,100 different
Irish airs now in print,’’ and he intimates that he could
himself, from manuscripts now in his possession, easily
run the number up to 5,000. ~ Assuredly no other
country under the sun, save the Little Green Isle, can
show such a treasure-store of native music—a mine of
wealth undoubtedly for Irish composers yet to be, when
our musical sons and daughters have learnt the art of
building upon past tradition a glorious Irish Tone-school
of the future.

Throughout the volumes the melodies are given
without accompaniment of any kind, as in keeping
with the instruments for which they are intended.
Differences of opinion will always exist as to how such
music, and particularly the ‘‘traditional '’ variety,
should be accompanied. Mr. Roche, in his Preface,
refers to the ““ glides,’’ or ‘‘ inflected intervals ’’ used
by the old country fiddlers, and mentions the sliding of
the player’s finger ““ about a quarter tone to a position
slightly sharper than its normal one in the diatonic
scale.””  Those who have heard the country fiddle at
fairs and elsewhere will understand the effect
described. 1t is the old story of the natural as against
the “ tempered " scale; or rather, the choice of scale-
intervals out of the vast gamut of natural harmonics
which leads, sooner or later, to the great points, or
nodes, of motion and rest known respectively in musical
parlance as dominant and tonic. But on these matters
volumes might be ‘written, and there would still be
some found to disagree as to the best adaptable
intervals; so the least said on that debatable subject
the better, lest we tread upon anyone’s pet corn.

Father Brennan, however, in his admirable ‘“ Fore-
word "’ to Mr. Roche’s Collection, puts the whole
question into a nutshell, showing at once his tolerance
of divergent opinions on the matter of so-called
*“ traditional ** musie and his foresight of the lines upon
which Irish music can alone be made available to the
weneral public and how it also might form the basis
of a school of national music yet {o come. He
speaks with knowledge of scales, and of Irish scales in
particular, noting their characteristics with lucidity,

July 14.—First indications of the enemy.  About
11.80 a.mn. a bee-hive was reported (by wireless) on the

carried out, thirteen men of the 4th Light Infantry
were severely wounded. With superhuman valour the
Heavy Artillery dashed up till almost within twenty
yards of the position and the vicious animals were cut
off to a man (bee), being annihilated after a desperate
cannonade of two hours. The wounded were sent back
by ambulance with an escort of five hundred men.
(The brave fellows with tears in their eyes entreated to
be allowed. to remain!) At three o’clock the wireless
again announced that we had come into contact with
the enemy—this time in the van. A gigantic he-goat
had taken up a position in the vicinity of a cross-roads.
After some hours’ hot action, in which Lieutenant
Barmy and Corporal Gunpowder, with several privates

ments one of the monster’s horns was found to be about

July 21.—Fling cur gallant lads into line. Get up
* on a barrel and tell them what they are expected to do.
They are face to face to-day with the power of Rome :
her trenches gape in their van, hungry to swallow their
thousands, thirsty to drink their blood. But let the
sons of the men of the Boyne and Derry fight for the
right their fathers bled for, let them bare tneir breasts
{o the battle, let them die—; here a scout came with the
8 intelligence that there were no papist-lined trenches to
: be seen in,the district, only some men cutting turf.
Tlalt for the day in disgust.

July 22.—\lore disaster:. News came of football
match between Celtic Rovers and Belfast Distillery.
Bight thousand men immediately threw away their
rifles and made for the nearest railway station. The
Farmers’ Corps are looking glum. They say if it does
not rain within three days something (I forget the name
of it) will be ruined. Our officers in tears at their stu-
pidity.  No spark of martial ardour remains in the
hearts of the once Grand Army of Ulster. Oh for a
roaring battery of T.ong Toms that I might rush head-
long upon it and die as befits the leader of a deserted
cause !

July 23.—Hear in a letter from home that my wife
has given away my second best pair of everyday pants
to a Belfast tramp. Will take the first train home in
the morning.  These parasites will {rade their true-

| owe blueness for my wardrobe no more! (Hear also that a
strong force of the Galway National Constabulary—
nearly fifty it is said—-are coming to investigate the
nature of our expedition. But of course Carson will
be able to manage without me.)

The rest of the diary has no immediate reference to
the Cork Expedition. It appears, however, that when
Captain Craig got home he somehow forgot all about
the Grand Army of Ulster. When he went back to
look for it some weeks after it had totally aisappeared.
Nobody seemed to know what had become of it, but
history whispers that quite a lot of men with bandaged
heads went northwards by train somewhere about that
time and that the Galway Constabulary had something
i to do with it. Captain Craig, after the events recorded
here, retired from public life, and began to take an in-
terest in aviation, wireless telegraphy and modern
civilisation generally.

With regard to the transfer of a *“ Reserved Service,”’
the formal procedure will be a resolution passed by

! transfer, and giving twelve months’ notice.

 members of the Imperial House shall be supplemented

In addition, therefore, to the forty-two permanent

(b) To prevent friction.

Tt will be arranged that the departments of the Im-
perial Parliament in Ireland—for example, the admini~_
stration of the ‘‘ Reserved Services '—will be superin-
tended and managed generally by Irish officers. ’

Again, although the Irish members of the Imperial
Parliament will remain subject to Imperial election laws
and writs, the elections will be superintended and
managed generally by Irish officers.

But responsibility for such arrangements—should
mistakes arise—will be undertaken by the lmperial
Parliament. 2 .

The buildings and property of the Imperial Govern-
ment in Ireland will be transferred to the Irish Govern-
ment without charge.

All laws and authorities, in existence at the time of
the passage of the Bill, will remain in force unless
altered, de facto by the Bill.

For the purpose of bringing the Act into operation
and removing minor difficulties, the Privy Council shall
be empowered to issue special orders.

As an instance, the Privy Council will regulate the
relations between the two Post Offices in respect of
foreign services.

And again, will secure the rights of interested per-
sons when any of the ‘“ Reserved Services’’ are being
transferred.

Such orders of the Privy Council will remain apen
to Parliamentary inspection, in the British House of
Parliament, for forty days, and unless objected to will
be put into operation.

(¢) The Judicial Power.

All Judges, whether of the High Courts, the county
or city courts, and Resident Magistrates will be ap-
pointed by the Irish Executive.
Thus the adjuncts and officers of the Irish Judiciary
will pass under the control of the Irish Parliament.
The Land Courts and the Land Commissionership
are also included.

Judicial matters in relation to Land Purchase are
alone excluded.
All Trish judges and all officers connacied with the
Trish Judiciary shall continue to receive the same sala- =~
ries and continue to perform the same duties or
analogous duties, as at present.

Portions of the Irish Judiciary, or officerships von-
nected therewith, may be reduced in importance or
even abolished, as soon as existing holders retire.

(@) Appeals—ordinary.

Appeals from the Irish High Courts to the British
House of Lords shall cease.

Appeals from the Irish High Courts to the Judicial
Comnmittee of the King’s Privy Council shall be sub-
stituted.

In the case of such appeals the customary Lovds of
Appeal must be supplemented by at least one represen-
tative from the Supreme Court of Ireland.

validity of an lrish Act, are also appealable to the
Judicial Committee.

But claims to Irish peerages will be decided, not by
the Judicial Committee, but by the House of Lords.

(f) The Imperial Veto.

The Veto proposals will have alveady suggested them-
selves.

The function of Vetoes is to demonstrate the theory
of the continued supremacy of the Imperial Parlia-
ment over all subordinate Parliaments. They are:—

A—The British Executive shall be empowered to
either (1) withhold, or (2) postpone the assent
of the King to Irish Acts.

B—Acts of the Irish Parliament shall be read sub-
ject to Acts of the Imperial Parliament, when-
ever there is a likelihood of conflict.

C—The Judicial Committec of the Privy Council—
outlined above—will also serve as a theoretic
check on Irish Acts.

(g) Civil Servants.

Permanent Civil Servants, whether on the Consoli-
dated Fund or the Estimates, shall continue to receive
the same salaries and continue to perform the same
duties or analogous duties, as at present.

Temporary Civil Servants will be circumstanced as
at present.

A Civil Service Committee is to be established.

ARE many Ivishmen following the uctivities of the

2 sense of social responsibility, to spread information
on social evils, such as sweating, bad housing, and rouse
a public conscience on the duty of alleviating them and
to instruct in Catholic principles of social rights and:
dutlgs nd Catholic programmes of reform. the evil
conditions of labour and of the poor that prevail in in-
dustriul centres are to be removed and justice to all
classes to be secured it can only be by the adoption of
this Catholic sccial programme in commercial life and
in legislation.
It is one of the compensations of our want of great
fuctory industries that social conditions are not so bad
in Treland; but even here there would be advantage in
discussion of sacial problems, such as the relations and
interests of employers and labourers, housing, the pro-
per care of the poor helpless, and the prevention of
pauperism in the rising generation.

TFather Slater, S.J., an earnest worker in the English

Vincent de Paul Society, but this and Father Parkin-
son’s (the President of the Guild) earlier lectures should
help to maie this scheme of work familiar and {o win
for it our sympathetic interest.

Later, we hope the example may induce a desire for
an Irish movement on somewhat similar lines.
